summary: Gruber responds to Steven Berlin Johnson's critique that the Apple Watch digital crown button is confusing by arguing that its behavior makes intuitive sense once you understand Apple Watch as having two modes: watch mode and app mode. He acknowledges Johnson's complaint has surface-level validity but argues that after extended use, the crown's context-sensitive behavior feels natural. Gruber's key framework is that the crown either takes you home within the current mode or switches you to the other mode. He agrees with only one of Johnson's specific complaints — that the crown shouldn't act as a 'back' button in notification detail views. He also explains why seemingly intuitive alternatives like global glance access wouldn't work given the watch's tiny display.
key_points: Apple Watch is best understood as having two modes: watch mode (for quick glances at information) and app mode (for doing things), with the crown button switching between them
The crown's context-sensitive behavior — returning you to wherever you came from — sounds confusing on paper but works well in practice, likely refined through extensive play-testing
The only change Gruber would make is removing the crown's 'back' button function in notification detail views, since that conflicts with its role as a 'go home' button
Global swipe-up for glances wouldn't work because the display is too small to distinguish between scrolling gestures and edge gestures
The side button operates outside both modes entirely, providing consistent behavior regardless of context
money_quotes: It's best to think of Apple Watch as having two modes: watch mode, and app mode.
Watch mode is where you take quick glances at information and notifications; app mode is where you go to 'do something'.
This sounds confusing. And if you're expecting Apple Watch's digital crown button to work like iOS's home button, it is not the expected behavior. But in practice, I think it works very well.
I suspect this arrangement wasn't designed in advance but was instead the result of many months of play-testing by the designers on the Apple Watch team.
'Back' just doesn't feel right for the digital crown button. It should simply mean go home in the current mode, or, if you're already home, switch to the other mode.
You don't have to 'clean up' and go back to the watch face manually. It just happens automatically when you stop using the watch.
You'd find yourself scrolling when you wanted to bring up Glances and bringing up Glances when you wanted to scroll. It'd be maddening.
key_insight: The Apple Watch digital crown's seemingly complex behavior becomes intuitive once you understand it through the lens of two modes — watch mode and app mode — where the crown simply means 'go home' or 'switch modes.'
